frameworks.name: linear dynamical systems
frameworks.one_line: Linear input-driven dynamics fit to PFC population responses within each context.

frameworks.name: context-dependent recurrent network dynamics
frameworks.one_line: Selection via context-modulated recurrent dynamics with transient input amplification.

frameworks.name: input-modulation / attentional gating
frameworks.one_line: Selection via subtle contextual modulation of sensory inputs upstream of PFC.

core_question: How does prefrontal cortex implement context-dependent selection and integration of sensory inputs from its population dynamics?
key_assumptions: PFC population responses can be approximated by linear dynamics driven by external inputs within a context
Mechanism can be inferred by fitting dynamical models to recorded population activity
manipulations: Behavioral context (which sensory feature is relevant for the decision)
Model class: linear-constrained vs unconstrained dynamics; recurrent-modulation vs input-modulation accounts
key_findings: Linear input-driven models capture PFC within-context responses comparably to unconstrained models
Two mechanisms — context-dependent recurrent dynamics with transient amplification, and contextual input modulation — fit the data equally well
Both accounts reveal input and recurrent properties missed by simpler descriptions of PFC responses
interpretations: Selection/integration may be implemented either by PFC recurrent context-switching or by upstream attentional input modulation
Linear-approximation modeling provides a principled framework linking rich population dynamics to neural computation
limitations_noted: Two distinct mechanisms remain equally consistent with the data — the approach does not adjudicate between them
